steps.title: Condition for infinitely many solutions
steps.content:
A system of two linear equations has infinitely many solutions when the equations are identical — same slope and same y-intercept.
Given: $y = \dfrac{2}{7}x + 3$. The second equation $y = mx + b$ must satisfy $m = \dfrac{2}{7}$ and $b = 3$.

steps.title: Test all four choices for b
steps.content:
• "$-3$": $b = -3$ gives $y = \frac{2}{7}x - 3$. Different y-intercept from the first equation ($3 \ne -3$) — parallel lines, no solutions. ✗
  • "$-\frac{1}{3}$": $b = -\frac{1}{3}$ gives $y = \frac{2}{7}x - \frac{1}{3}$. Different y-intercept — parallel, no solutions. ✗
  • "$\frac{1}{3}$": $b = \frac{1}{3}$ gives $y = \frac{2}{7}x + \frac{1}{3}$. Different y-intercept — parallel, no solutions. ✗
  • "$3$": $b = 3$ gives $y = \frac{2}{7}x + 3$, identical to the first equation. Every point on the line is a solution — infinitely many solutions. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
